strengths: High median household income relative to Top Market average, spurred by higher-paying tech sector
Strong university presence and college-educated population
Highest desirability score — indicates continued favorability for the region
weaknesses: Large, recent increase in cost of living reducing affordability
Low supply barriers in fringe submarkets; among highest intermediate-term supply growth
Legislation increasing ability to develop homes may reduce multifamily advantage

rankings.cat: Best American City to Live In

realpage.v: Deliveries falling 30,236 (2024) → 16,964 (2025) → 10,153 (2026F). Supply collapse enables reacceleration.
First year since 2022 where projected absorption (19,425) > deliveries (10,153). Occupancy recovery accelerates.
Pflugerville/Wells Branch, Round Rock/Georgetown have 0 UC units — prime acquisition profile.
Metro job growth 0.9% YoY Nov-25, forecast to 2.0% in 2026F. D/S ratio recovers from 0.4x to 1.3x.
Build avg 2009 vs national 1998 — classic value-add plays limited. Focus on stabilized core-plus/distress.
9.5% of asking rent ($143/u) market-wide 4Q25 — 27th highest nationally. Monitor for burn-off.

apartmentTrends.v: WMS absorbed 514 units in 4Q25 with 149 delivered — strong net positive absorption.
Cedar Park/Leander 16.1% concessions (3rd highest). Pricing compressed — value opportunity.
432 Class A props in universe; 110 new completed in past year still absorbing. Stabilized vs lease-up is the key A-class split.
Properties priced <$2.00/SF absorbed 112% of new deliveries. Above that threshold, absorption lags.
2025 new starts ~7,000 vs 2024 ~17,000 (-60%). Permits -22%. Creates 2027-2028 supply gap.
9,000+ new jobs announced in electronics, energy, software, semiconductors — high-wage demand.

newmark.v: Pop growth 2.1-2.8% annually — among fastest large metros. 2030 adds 153,650 people / 152,159 jobs.
Median HHI $99,660, #1 city for wage growth 2018-2023. Rent-to-income 15.6% vs 30% affordable threshold.
0% corporate + personal income tax. CODB +6.7% vs US but below SF/NY/LA — net positive employer attraction.
Office-using jobs +31.4% 5yr (#2 nationally). Industrial +17.7% (#5). Both direct MF demand anchors.
Retail vacancy among tightest in US — signals organic sub-level demand + desirability for suburban zones.
Newmark 92.4% occ at $1,459. RealPage 92.6%. ApartmentTrends 88.5% (CNV-only). Stabilized vs lease-up split.

UC_DEALS.n: Four Seasons Private Residences Lake Austin

EXEC_NARRATIVE.lead: Austin is a Green Street grade-A market, ranked 9th of 50 top U.S. multifamily markets. It is coming off a historic supply shock: 30,236 new units were delivered in 2024, the peak of a multi-year building wave. That surge pushed market-wide concessions to elevated levels and dragged effective rent growth down. By 26Q1, ERG had moderated to -5.1% YoY and is forecast to turn positive by 27Q3 as the absorption window opens. Forward new-unit deliveries collapse to 10,271 in calendar 2026 (a 66% drop from peak) and 8,233 in 2027 (CoStar Q1 vintage forecast). 2026 becomes the first year since 2022 where absorption (the count of units actually leased) exceeds new supply — projected 12,702 absorbed against 10,271 delivered (calendar 2026 forecast). This is a rent-growth inflection point, not a structural market decline.
EXEC_NARRATIVE.thesis: The investment case rests on four structural drivers. Austin ranks #1 of 50 top markets in Green Street's 5-year population growth forecast, #1 in wage growth and job growth in the Milken Institute Best-Performing Cities Index (2018-2023), and #2 in Market Revenue Per Available Foot (M-RevPAF, a core multifamily revenue productivity metric) growth forecast. Office-using employment — jobs in sectors like technology, finance, and professional services — grew 31.4% over five years, second-highest nationally. Median household income of $99,660 against $1,529 market-average asking rent yields a 16.4% rent-to-income ratio, well within affordability bands that support rent recovery. Texas' 0% state corporate and personal income tax keeps employer attraction structural.
EXEC_NARRATIVE.timing: The absorption picture depends on which question gets asked. Austin's combined pipeline is 22,649 units in active lease-up (73 properties at 57% weighted occupancy) plus 15,918 UC units (48 deals delivering 26Q2 through 28Q2) — 38,567 total units. At a conservative 8 leases/month per property and 15% pre-leased on UC, market clearing unfolds in stages: supply overhang meaningfully resolves (75% of pool cleared) by 28Q2 — this is the investment-actionable milestone. Market is functionally healed (85% cleared) by 29Q1, with concessions compressing and rent growth reaccelerating. Full 95% stab runs to 34Q2 but is dominated by a handful of late-delivery large UCs (Colony Park 831u, AMLI Park Central 650u) — the full-stab number shouldn't anchor the thesis on its own. At observed median velocity (11.6/mo), the 75% clearing milestone pulls forward to 27Q3-27Q4. Highest-conviction pockets where absorption math clears fastest: Round Rock (lone LU at 93% occ, zero UC — already clearing), Downtown Austin (3 LU at 64% occ with strong 32/mo velocity, 3 UC delivering), Hill Country, and Riverside. Mid-conviction with structural overhang: Cedar Park, Pflugerville, Georgetown-Leander. Avoid until absorption clears: Northeast Austin (13 LU + 9 UC = 8,218 units overhang).

RISK_FACTORS.label: Supply Overshoot Extension
RISK_FACTORS.detail: 2026 projected new-unit deliveries of 10,153 assume the recent collapse in new project starts holds. If deferred 2024 projects re-enter the pipeline, effective rent growth recovery slips to 2027. Monitor CoStar/RealPage monthly starts data — a Q2 or Q3 2026 print above 4,000 units breaks the thesis.

RISK_FACTORS.label: Tech Concentration Cyclicality
RISK_FACTORS.detail: Austin ranks #1 in wage growth partly because the technology sector pays premium wages — and tech had net job losses in 2023-24 during the sector-wide downturn. A second tech downturn would hit Austin rent growth before any other U.S. metro. Monitor Samsung (Taylor semiconductor fabrication plant), Tesla (Gigafactory), and Apple (North Austin campus) hiring announcements as leading indicators.

RISK_FACTORS.label: Interest Rate Environment
RISK_FACTORS.detail: Going-in capitalization rates (annual net operating income as a percentage of purchase price) at 5.7% market-wide against the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield near 4.0% give approximately a 150 basis point spread — thin for multifamily by historical standards. An additional 50 basis point move higher on rates reprices the market down another 8-12%. Underwriting should assume exit cap rates of 5.5-6.0%, not 5.0%.

RISK_FACTORS.label: Property Tax & Insurance
RISK_FACTORS.detail: Texas property tax is not deductible at the ownership-entity level and runs 2.2-2.5% of assessed property value annually. Insurance premiums in central Texas rose 30-45% following Winter Storm Uri (February 2021). Both line items materially affect net operating income (property revenue less operating expenses) and are harder to hedge than rent.

RISK_FACTORS.label: Migration Moderation
RISK_FACTORS.detail: Population growth of 2.1-2.8% annually is among the highest of all top-50 U.S. metros but has moderated from the 2021-22 surge. If net migration (people moving in minus people moving out) drops below 50,000 per year, absorption forecasts compress and the 2026 rent recovery slows. Monitor U-Haul one-way rental volume and major van-line carrier data as high-frequency indicators.

SUB_NARRATIVES.Downtown Austin: Highest $/u rent in the metro at $3,568 with 87.4% occupancy. 1,254u delivered T12 against 1,023u absorbed — slightly long but improving as recent vintage stabilizes. 352u still under construction (Waterline). Premium urban core, deepest concession exposure.
SUB_NARRATIVES.Central Austin: 3,971 units of inventory at 91.2% occupancy with 0 UC. Class B properties benefit from UT anchor demand and established walkability. Tightest UC pipeline among urban subs — supply protection on a 5-year horizon.
SUB_NARRATIVES.West Austin: Small inventory (2,152u) but best operational fundamentals in metro: 6.4% vacancy, slight positive ERG. Zero pipeline, premium grade (Green Street A++, cap rate 4.1%). Highest-barrier submarket.
SUB_NARRATIVES.South Austin: 20,335 units of inventory at 88.7% occupancy. Class A concessions running 13.2%. South Congress corridor retains brand pricing power; outer South Austin shares SE/SW oversupply dynamics.
SUB_NARRATIVES.North Austin: 31,903 units of inventory — largest suburban pool — with 1,481 UC and Apple HQ employment anchor. 13.5% vacancy reflects recent deliveries; A/D ratio of 3.51 signals strong absorption coverage.
SUB_NARRATIVES.Northwest Austin: 35,524 units of inventory with 586 UC. Green Street submarket grade A. Tech employer cluster (Dell/Apple corridor) drives demand; 10.8% vacancy at 9% concessions reflects lease-up momentum.
SUB_NARRATIVES.Southwest Austin: Green Street A++ submarket with highest median household income ($193K) in metro. 11.1% vacancy reflects 803u delivered T12 against 220u absorbed — supply digestion underway. 1,089 UC with $1,708 rent and -4.3% ERG. Wait for absorption to catch up before deploying.
SUB_NARRATIVES.Round Rock: Thesis submarket: 21,944 units of inventory, 0 UC, 10.4% vacancy with 2.70x absorption/delivery ratio. Dell Technologies HQ. RealPage ranks #16 for current ERG but projects +3.3% in 2026F.
SUB_NARRATIVES.Cedar Park: 15,829 units of inventory, 0 UC, 9.9% vacancy. Concessions at 16.1% (3rd highest in market) — pricing has compressed, creating value opportunity as supply pressure exhausts.
SUB_NARRATIVES.Pflugerville: 24,269 units of inventory, 0 UC, 14.2% vacancy with 1.12x A/D. Classic suburban zero-pipeline thesis zone. Pre-2000 stock minimal — most inventory is 2010+ vintage.
SUB_NARRATIVES.Georgetown-Leander: 17,489 units inventory with 574 UC. 15.2% vacancy reflects the recent pipeline wave. 2.18x A/D ratio suggests strong absorption ahead of the stabilization curve.
SUB_NARRATIVES.Far West Austin: Small submarket (149 units in CoStar) with no UC and positive ERG. Lake Travis adjacency drives premium pricing. Limited transaction volume; institutional-grade but supply-constrained.
SUB_NARRATIVES.Lake Travis: 3,978 units inventory at 88.7% occupancy. Premium recreational submarket with 0 UC. Cap rates tightest in region (4.3-4.5% per Green Street). Thin deal flow.
SUB_NARRATIVES.Riverside: 5,814 units inventory at 92.2% occupancy. Eastside access to Downtown and airport. 1.4% UC ratio. Green Street grade A.
SUB_NARRATIVES.East Austin: 19,201 units inventory, 2,328 UC, 12.3% vacancy. Highest development activity in metro. 2.08x A/D ratio. Transit corridor exposure (Project Connect) creates long-term optionality.
SUB_NARRATIVES.Southeast Austin: 16,442 units inventory, 1,259 UC, 18.8% vacancy. Weak current operating metrics but Tesla Gigafactory proximity drives 2027+ thesis. 0.85x A/D suggests absorption lagging deliveries.
SUB_NARRATIVES.Northeast Austin: 19,601 units inventory, 2,234 UC, 20.6% vacancy — among weakest current operating metrics. Pipeline still arriving. Avoid until A/D ratio recovers above 1.0x.
SUB_NARRATIVES.Midtown Austin: 17,027 units inventory, 1,681 UC, 12% vacancy. 3.2x A/D ratio signals strong absorption. Mixed-use corridor with The Domain anchor supporting rent premiums.
SUB_NARRATIVES.San Marcos: 10,978 units inventory with 807 UC, 21.1% vacancy. Texas State University drives demographic skew; high student housing exposure. T12 absorption (1,091u) running 1.8x deliveries (600u) — improving but pipeline still building. Cap rate 6.3% reflects risk pricing.
SUB_NARRATIVES.Far North Austin: 4,004 units inventory, 511 UC, 23.8% vacancy — highest vacancy in metro and pipeline tripled this quarter. Fringe position with low barriers to development. T12 deliveries (889u) outpacing absorption (774u). Pass until vacancy below 15% and UC drops materially.
SUB_NARRATIVES.South Central Austin: 13,667 units inventory, 572 UC. Strong operating fundamentals (Green Street A-). SoCo corridor and Zilker Park adjacency support premium positioning.
SUB_NARRATIVES.Buda-Kyle: Fast-growing suburban corridor south of Austin. Limited institutional inventory but top-5 Market Stadium forecast score. Thesis: hold-to-stabilize as population growth outpaces supply.
SUB_NARRATIVES.Caldwell County: 932 units inventory, 27.9% vacancy, -10.4% annual ERG — worst submarket by every operating metric. Thin institutional universe. Pass.
SUB_NARRATIVES.Hill Country: Dispersed submarket with limited multifamily inventory. Green Street Far Southwest submarket (grade A+). Low deal flow; institutional activity concentrated in 3-4 specific pockets.
SUB_NARRATIVES.Bastrop County: 7 institutional properties in CoStar universe. Tesla Gigafactory proximity drives emerging thesis but current operating metrics reflect small-sample volatility. Monitor.

DV.props: CoStar property-level data, refreshed May 2026. All occupancy, vacancy, concession, and rent fields reflect May 2026 snapshot.
DV.at: ApartmentTrends snapshot, Q4 2025. CNV (conventional) universe only.
DV.ms: Market Stadium zip data, 2025 vintage. Annual refresh cycle.
DV.sales: CoStar Austin sales data, Jan 2020 – Q1 2026 (467 transactions).
DV.ts: CoStar quarterly time series. Actuals through 26Q1 (after reconciliation with the May 2026 Source of Truth snapshot); 20-quarter forecasts through 31Q1. Today marked at 26Q2.
DV.ann: Annual deliveries/absorption. Actuals 2016–2025; forecasts 2026–2030. 2026 is the first crossover year.
DV.score: Composite score blends Submarket 25% / Zip 40% / Property 35% with 16 zip factors, 7 property factors, 4 submarket factors. BUY ≥ 65, WATCH 50–64, AVOID <50.
DV.subNarr: Submarket narratives updated quarterly. Current vintage: Q1 2026. Next refresh: Q2 2026 results.
